“Why is no one ready?” he asks.
Dismukes, who changed into a dapper suit, then shows up holding two drinks that look almost exactly the same.
“James, which color drink do you want to throw in people’s faces? It’s a tough call. They’re so different,” he says, prompting Rodrigo to laugh.

The moment is reminiscent of an iconic scene from “The Devil Wears Prada,” where Anne Hathaway’s character, Andy, laughs as one of Priestly’s colleagues holds up two belts that look nearly identical and says, “It’s a tough call. They’re so different.”
Johnson isn’t amused by Rodrigo’s giggle and says, “Is something funny, Elizabeth?”
“Oh, no, no. It’s just my first time hosting, so I’m still learning about all these skits,” she replies.
Johnson then channels his inner Miranda Priestly and launches into a monologue similar to the cerulean blue speech the character gave Andy in the film.

“‘These skits’? OK, you think this show has nothing to do with you, pop star? Take yourself too seriously to do sketch comedy. But what you don’t know is that your breakout role in ‘High School Musical: The Musical: The Series’ was based on ‘High School Musical’ starring Zac Efron, was it not? Who did a sketch in 2009 about how spontaneously bursting into song isn’t normal anywhere but East High,” he says.
Johnson goes on to describe how Rodrigo’s song “Drivers License” inspired an “SNL” sketch about a “group of big, strong men” who “are secretly Olivia Rodrigo fans.”
“So I find it sort of comical that you think these ‘skits’ have nothing to do with you, when in fact your entire career has been turned into sketches by the people in this very room,” he says.
The scene shifts when Brennan reappears and warns that “SNL” showrunner Lorne Michaels is approaching: “And touchdown! Lorne just got here.”
Johnson’s confident demeanor crumbles as he addresses Rodrigo, saying, “Ah, crap! Please don’t tell Lorne about this. Run!”
As Johnson, Dismukes and Wickline run off, Johnson shouts, “Sorry, Elizabeth!”
Rodrigo says, “It’s Olivia!”
“OK. Hi Olivia,” he replies.

title: Queen Camilla Gifts Replica Roo to New York Public Library, Completing Their Winnie-the-Pooh Collection
summary: Queen Camilla gifted the New York Public Library with a replica Roo doll, completing their Winne-the-Pooh collection.
story: It was a special day for the New York Public Library, as their Winnie-the-Pooh and Friends collection got slightly bigger.

Amid her and King Charles III's visit to the United States, Queen Camilla took some time to deliver a heartfelt gift to the New York City library. On April 29, the queen presented a new Roo doll that was added to the library's display dedicated to the beloved children's book.
The Roo replica comes almost 40 years after the Winnie-the-Pooh, Piglet, Tigger, Eeyore and Kanga dolls were donated to the library.

The five dolls belonged to the real-life Christopher Robin, who inspired the characters in A.A. Milne’s books “Winnie-the-Pooh” (1926) and “The House at Pooh Corner” (1928), according to the library. After arriving in the U.S. in 1947, the dolls were first displayed in Milne’s American publisher E.P. Dutton's offices before making their way to the library in 1987.
However, missing from the bunch was the baby kangaroo stuffed animal, which was lost in an apple orchard during the 1930s, the library notes.

“People from all over the world visit every year to meet the real Winnie-the-Pooh and friends, which are on free and permanent display for all to see in the Library’s Polonsky Exhibition,” President and CEO of the New York Public Library Anthony W Marx said in a press release.

“On behalf of the Library and our millions of visitors, we thank Her Majesty Queen Camilla — and we welcome Roo to New York City,” he continued. “Roo will help us continue to share the wonder and magic of this timeless story with readers of all ages for generations to come.”

Camilla also took a moment to read “Winne-the-Pooh” to a group of children during her visit. It's a special milestone for the book, which is celebrating its 100th birthday after being first published on October 14, 1926.

The royal couple is currently on their first official state visit as Britain’s reigning monarch and queen consort. They arrived in Washington D.C. on April 27, stopping in New York, before heading to Virginia to celebrate America’s 250th anniversary of independence.

Camilla's stop at the New York Public Library celebrated both the U.S. and United Kingdom's love of literature, according to a royal press release. While there, the queen also spoke with Jenna Bush Hager about her time in the U.S. and appreciation for reading.
The exclusive conversation will air in full on TODAY April 30.

title: Woman Who Inspired Emily From 'The Devil Wears Prada' Says the Book Felt Like 'A Betrayal'
summary: Leslie Fremar, a celebrity stylist, reveals she's the inspiration for the character of Emily from the 2003 book and 2006 film, "The Devil Wears Prada."
story: Anna Wintour famously inspired the role of Meryl Streep's Miranda Priestly in "The Devil Wears Prada," while the experience of her second assistant, Anne Hathaway's Andy Sachs, was based on the book's author Lauren Weisberger.
But the inspiration for Emily, the desperate and snobby first assistant, has long been a mystery. Until now.

In a new interview with Vogue editor-in-chief Chloe Malle, celebrity stylist Leslie Fremar identified herself as the inspiration for Emily, a character played by Emily Blunt in the film and its upcoming sequel.

Fremar, who previously worked as both a junior and first assistant to Wintour, appeared on Vogue's podcast, "The Run-Through," to discuss how she really feels about Weisberger's bestselling book, which was published in 2003, and the hit movie.
“I don’t think I’ve ever really talked about it,” Fremar said during the podcast, later insisting that she knows she was the character's inspiration.
“She’s me — I am Emily," she added.
Fremar now has a successful career as a fashion stylist and has worked with Charlize Theron for years.
But in 1990s, she worked as Wintour's assistant and later hired Weisberger. They worked together for less than a year, Fremar said.

Weisberger went on to write a book based on her experiences at the fashion magazine. By the time "The Devil Wears Prada" was published, Fremar was no longer working directly for Wintour. However, she got a call from her former boss when Wintour first heard about the book.

"I went into her office and she said, ‘Who’s Lauren Weisberger?' And I said, ‘She was your junior assistant. She was only here for a short period of time, maybe eight months.' And she’s like, ‘Well, she wrote a book about us, and you’re worse than me,’” Fremar recalled.
Wintour let Fremar read her advance copy of the book, which the stylist described as "quite mean." She also suggested that an editor had "softened" the book before it was published.

"There wasn't this lightness to it. It felt quite dark, I remember thinking. And I found that quite hurtful. I think what got put into the world is a much lighter, nicer version of what she actually wrote," Fremar said.
The stylist said the book felt like "a betrayal" at the time.
"Even though someone obviously advised her to make it fiction, it was really based off of a lot of things that, you know, I lived, she lived," she said.
When asked what her interactions with Weisberger were like, Fremar acknowledged that she may not have always been kind while they were working together.

"I probably was not very nice, and I probably was high-strung because I felt like I was having to do her job as well," Fremar said. "So for me, that was really frustrating. I think she was probably just sitting there writing a book and not necessarily taking the job as seriously as I did or a hundred million girls would."
On that note, Fremar said she knows she inspired one popular line from the film.

"I definitely told her a million girls would kill for the job," she said. "That was definitely my line because I actually really believed that, and I knew that she didn’t necessarily want to be there."
Fremar confirmed she has seen the movie and said it did a “great job of fictionalizing” the world she worked in for so long.

"I think people that knew me at the time, even PR (professionals) or (anyone) working at brands, they all knew that it was me. But it was fictionalized, so I was able, mentally, to separate myself from it where I didn't feel like it was this horrible betrayal of me, per se," she said.
Fremar said she has not spoken to Weisberger since the latter left Vogue.
In an essay for Vogue published April 28, Weisberger reflected on writing "The Devil Wears Prada" and said if she wrote it today, it would be "more layered."

"I have more empathy now — for the assistants and the bosses, for the 20-somethings trying to prove themselves, and for those who already have. That kind of understanding comes only with time, experience, and a few hard-earned recalibrations," Weisberger wrote.

"Still, the version of me who wrote that book had something I cannot easily access now: unfiltered honesty. There is a boldness that comes from being young and outraged," she added.You’re less careful, less diplomatic, not as concerned with the consequences. And there is real power in that."
When asked what a run-in with the author would be like today, Fremar said “very awkward.” Still, Fremar underscored the fact that she doesn’t “hold a grudge” towards her former colleague.
“There’s nothing to be said,” Fremar said.

title: Meryl Streep Reveals She First Turned Down ‘Devil Wears Prada.’ Then She Asked to Double Her Pay
summary: Meryl Streep tells Jenna Bush Hager that she first turned down her role in 2006's "The Devil Wears Prada" — and then she asked for double her pay.
story: “The Devil Wears Prada” without Meryl Streep is like girding without loins, but it almost happened.

The original 2006 film has become a classic, earning two Academy Award nominations, including a best actress nod for Streep, who won raves for her portrayal as icy Runway editor Miranda Priestly. The role, based on famed Vogue editor Anna Wintour, also netted Streep a Golden Globe Award for best performance by an actress in a motion picture — comedy or musical.

Streep reprises the iconic role in “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” opening May 1. The three-time Oscar winner — who is actually a distant relative of Wintour — was not initially sold on the part, however, even though she had faith the movie would be a success.

“I knew it was going to be a hit,” she told Jenna Bush Hager in an interview alongside “The Devil Wears Prada” co-stars Anne Hathaway, Emily Blunt and Stanley Tucci — all of whom return for the sequel — that aired April 29 on TODAY.
“I read the script. Script was great. And they called me up and they made an offer, and I said, ‘No, not going to do it,’” Streep continued.
“Why?” an astonished Jenna asked.
Streep felt she could earn more than she was being offered.

“I knew it was going to be a hit, and I wanted to see if I doubled my ask,” she said. “And they went right away and said sure. And I thought, I’m 50, 60 — it took me this long to understand that I could do that.”
Streep sensed she would be vital to the film, even as landing the part proved to be a teachable moment for her.
“They needed me, I felt. ... I was ready to retire. But, you know, that was a lesson,” she said.
It’s been 20 years since the original film came out and the sequel, which features a cameo from Jenna, proves it’s better fashionably late than never.
“I think people are hungry for it,” Tucci told Jenna about the sequel. “Arguably, it has become a classic film. It endures, and people seem to have not been able to get enough of us. So here we are.”
As for the hype that comes with making a second film, Hathaway says that wasn’t necessarily the case in this instance.

“There’s always pressure when you make a movie, but because the screenplay was so amazing, I felt like that actually took a lot of the pressure off,” she said. “Then it was just back to what we do every time, which is just hang out and try to make a great script better.”

title: Jenna’s Cameo in 'Devil Wears Prada 2' Had Savannah and Hoda So Excited, They Nearly Missed It
summary: Savannah Guthrie talked about what it was like seeing Jenna Bush Hager's cameo in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
story: Jenna Bush Hager is getting rave reviews for her cameo in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
Jenna saw the highly anticipated sequel with Savannah Guthrie and Hoda Kotb, who were in awe of watching her on the big screen.

“She has this cameo. And then Hoda and I started, like, screaming and yelling, and we kind of missed the whole thing, because we were like, ‘There’s our girl!’” Savannah, sitting next to Jenna, told Craig Melvin and Carson Daly on TODAY April 29.
"If you blink, then you won't see me," Jenna added.
She then gave more details about her big moment in the film.

“I can't remember exactly what my line is. Something like, Meryl says, ‘Jenna Bush Hager, Andy Sachs,’” Jenna said, referring to Anne Hathaway’s character. “And I say, ‘Wow, nice to meet you. What a great interview,’ or something like that.”
While a brief moment, Savannah made sure everyone knew why Jenna's cameo is particularly notable.
"There’s some other cameos — she’s the only one who has a scene with Anne and Meryl,” Savannah said.
In addition to appearing in the film, Jenna interviewed "The Devil Wears Prada" stars who reunited for the sequel 20 years after the original — Hathaway, Meryl Streep, Emily Blunt and Stanley Tucci.
“I am in this film,” Jenna told them during the interview, which aired on TODAY April 29.
“Yes, you are!” the cast replied.
“You tried to steal my scene,” Streep joked.
“I had just arrived. And they were like, ‘Jenna.’ And I’m like, ‘Who? Me?’ ‘Meryl. Anne.’ I’m like, ‘Wait, what?' Because there was no script," Jenna recalled. "You know, I was prepared to be cut."
"Welcome to acting," Hathaway responded. "You do everything, you pour your heart and your soul into it and then you walk away and you go, 'I hope I'm in it.'"
"It was the fact that you all welcomed me so lovingly," Jenna added.
Speaking with her TODAY co-hosts, Jenna revealed how she really felt while filming a scene opposite Hathaway and Streep.
“When they said, ‘Jenna and Meryl,’ my knees started shaking,” Jenna said.
The original 2006 film has become a beloved classic, while garnering a pair of Academy Award nominations, including a best actress nod for Meryl Streep. Did that add any pressure to this follow up?

“There’s always pressure when you make a movie, but because the screenplay was so amazing, I felt like that actually took a lot of the pressure off,” Hathaway said. “Then it was just back to what we do every time, which is just hang out and try to make a great script better.”
“The Devil Wears Prada 2” opens in theaters May 1.

title: See Teaser Trailer for 'Ted Lasso' Season 4, Set for 2026 Premiere
summary: Ted Lasso is back to inspire another soccer team to greatness, except this time it's a women's squad looking to reach new heights.
story: Ted Lasso is back to inspire another soccer team to greatness, except this time it's a women's squad looking to reach new heights.

A teaser trailer for "Ted Lasso" Season 4 dropped on April 28 with a look at the upcoming season of the Emmy-winning Apple TV hit, which features the folksy American coach played by Jason Sudeikis guiding a squad of professional soccer players in England.

The trailer shows that just about all the familiar faces return, including Emmy winners Hannah Waddingham as team owner Rebecca Welton and Brett Goldstein as pugnacious former star Roy Kent. Juno Temple returns as publicist Keeley Jones, Brendan Hunt is back as Lasso's hilarious assistant coach and Jeremy Swift returns as the team's loveable oddball director of football operations.
Joining the cast for the upcoming season are Tanya Reynolds, Jude Mack, Faye Marsay, Rex Hayes, Aisling Sharkey, Abbie Hern and Grant Feely, according to Apple TV.
The new season will premiere Aug. 5 on Apple TV.

The trailer shows that Lasso has returned to Richmond, where he will be coaching a second division women's soccer team, hoping to inspire them to become a winning team like he previously did with a men's team in the city.
"Throughout the course of the season, Ted and the team learn to leap before they look, taking chances they never thought they would," the season's description reads.
Season 3, which aired in 2023, was previously billed as the “end of the story that we wanted to tell, that we were hoping to tell, that we loved to tell," Sudeikis told Deadline at the time.

Season 3 ended with Ted choosing to leave England and return home to Kansas City to be with his son. But the finale also teased the eventual Season 4's premise, when Keeley proposed an AFC Richmond women's soccer venture to Rebecca.

title: Val Kilmer’s Daughter Responds to Criticism of AI Performance, Says Late Dad Wanted to ‘Set Precedent’
summary: Val Kilmer’s daughter, Mercedes, explained why her family allowed an AI-generated version of the late actor to appear in the upcoming movie, "As Deep as the Grave."
story: Val Kilmer’s daughter explained why her family supports an AI-generated version of the late actor appearing in an upcoming movie, which has sparked a fierce debate in a Hollywood industry on edge about AI taking jobs from actors.

Mercedes Kilmer, 34, spoke on TODAY April 29 about an AI-generated version of the late star of "Top Gun," "The Doors" and "Tombstone" appearing as Father Fintan, a priest and Native American spiritualist in the upcoming movie "As Deep as the Grave."
She said her father "wanted to do this" out of a desire to create "structures for actors to own their licensing and to have rights."

“My dad was very passionate that this is the time, before these laws are written, to make sure that there’s a structure for compensation, to make sure that actors get paid on par with what they would get paid if they were physically doing it, and if it creates more jobs in that way, that’s wonderful,” Kilmer told Craig Melvin and Savannah Guthrie.

“I think this is a really historic precedent, and I’m really proud of him,” she added. “It started off as something to overcome the limitations of his illness, but then it evolved into something that he really was like, ‘Oh wait I have a chance to actually set precedent.’”
Val Kilmer spent 10 years being treated for throat cancer, which robbed him of his voice, before his death at 65 from pneumonia in April 2025.
Artificial intelligence was used to help re-create Kilmer's voice for a heart-wrenching reunion scene with Tom Cruise's Pete "Maverick" Mitchell in the 2022 sequel "Top Gun: Maverick."
The technology created Kilmer's character in "As Deep as the Grave" using recordings of his voice and archival footage and family photos.
“This is a character Val wanted to play. He was really clear about that before he passed,"  John Voorhees, one of the film’s producers, told “Entertainment Tonight" on April 16.
The filmmakers told "ET" they followed SAG-AFTRA's guidelines for "consent, compensation and collaboration" in the movie, which will come out later this year.

Mercedes Kilmer said on TODAY her father was initially offered “As Deep as the Grave” in 2018, but the project was stalled when the COVID pandemic began in 2020. He knew he wouldn’t be able to act in the film due to his health issues but wanted to be involved.

“The character speaks 1920s Navajo language, which is a language that they were able to sort of recover for the film, which is I think like a really ethical, interesting use of the technology,” his daughter said. “If we’re going to have this, we can make things that expand the possibilities of what we can do as humans, not replace them, if that makes sense.”
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google
The inclusion of the AI-generated Kilmer character sparked a fierce debate in a Hollywood climate where actors and others in the industry are fearful the technology will threaten their livelihoods.

“I have gotten some negative comments,” Kilmer said. “It’s kind of fallen into two camps — people that maybe have a more precarious position in the industry are worried and see AI as a threat, which is absolutely valid. Younger actors and musicians — I’m a musician and a lot of people I know, we’re so scared of this technology.

“And then at the same time, I’ve gotten a lot of really good responses from people maybe more established in the industry that see it as a way to protect the actors’ ownership of their IP," she added. "We have to contend with this technology one way or another and avoiding it is not necessarily the way. It’s much easier to structure the rights if you proactively license.”
Kilmer was asked if her family will use her father’s AI-generated image, voice and likeness for any future movies.

“No, because I wouldn’t just put his likeness in something without his permission, necessarily," she said. "There are just certain things that he talked about, ways to use to it, that we may do eventually."
She cited the example of using an AI version of the late actor for video games based on some of his famous characters from his career.
"Something like that could happen, and so now we know that we’re protected," she said.

Kilmer's role is not the first digital re-creation or AI-assisted performance in Hollywood. James Earl Jones allowed AI technology to replicate the voice of his iconic "Star Wars" character Darth Vader, the late Carrie Fisher was digitally resurrected in two "Star Wars" movies, and Harrison Ford was de-aged by AI in the most recent "Indiana Jones" movie.
Mercedes Kilmer was asked how this could affect the estates of late movie legends like Marilyn Monroe or James Dean as far as authorizing AI-generated versions of those icons for future works.

“I don’t know if I can speak to that because my dad knew about this project," she said. "This project gave us an opportunity to make sure if someone in the future uses his likeness unauthorized, we can say, ‘Oh no, look, this is what you’re supposed to do.'"

title: ‘Million Dollar Secret’ Season 2 Finale Recap: Winner Breaks Down Game-Winning Move
summary: Read a recap of the "Million Dollar Secret" Season 2 finale and find out what the winner reveals to TODAY.com about their masterful victory.
story: Warning: Spoilers ahead for “Million Dollar Secret” Season 2...
After constant scheming, secret agendas, a showmance and even a “clothespinning gate,” Season 2 of “Million Dollar Secret” has come to an end and the final millionaire has been revealed.

The Netflix competition series returned for its second season earlier this month and introduced 14 contestants who checked into The Stag, hoping to leave a million dollars richer. Throughout the episodes, one cast member was randomly chosen as the secret millionaire. That player would then privately meet with host Peter Serafinowicz, who would reveal an agenda that needed to be discreetly completed before the next elimination dinner. If the player was successful, the millionaire would gain an advantage that would help them hold onto the money. If they could not complete the task or didn’t attempt it, they would receive a penalty that would put them at risk.

During the season, the money jumped from player to player, with some managing to conceal their identity and others being sniffed out by their competitors. By the time the season finale arrived on Wednesday, April 29, only four contestants remained: Kat Ellis, Kaleb Moon, Lauren Gierth and Nick Pellecchia. To be crowned the winner, they had to survive two more guest activities, the last elimination dinner and the final showdown.

Who opened their box and found stacks of bills waiting for them at the end of the season? Read a recap of Episode 8, titled “Lie Hard,” and find out what the winner reveals to TODAY.com about their masterful victory, below.
What Happened in the ‘Million Dollar Secret’ Season 2 Finale?

At the start of the finale, Kaleb is still the millionaire after convincing the others to eliminate Daisy Skarning in Episode 7 so he could use his kill shot against Kevin Moranz. Host Peter explains that the final four will be divided into two pairs for a guest activity that tests their memory. One player in each team reads statements about eliminated players written on the back of puzzle pieces. After selecting the true statement, the other member in the pair has to use the piece to complete a puzzle underwater.

Thanks to Lauren’s detailed notebook, she and Nick defeat Kat and Kaleb. As the winners, they have to decide which contestant to send home. Kat uses the real clue she received during the final visit to the Trophy Room to her advantage. She and Kevin previously came up with a fictional clue to trick the rest of the players.
She tells TODAY.com that crafting that clue with Kevin took much longer than what was shown in the episode.
“I have been told we set a record for the longest Trophy Room for debating who got into the room and taking the time to make up the clue,” Kat says. “I think it was 45 minutes.”
She adds, “I’ve also been told that production takes months to come up with the clue. So they were just impressed that we got one at all. And it worked.”

The other players were fooled in Episode 7. But, in the penultimate episode when Kat tries to use the real clue — that the millionaire is a fashionista who previously owned a clothing store — she isn’t able to trick Kaleb into confirming he has the money. So, during the final elimination dinner, Lauren and Nick, who think Kat is the millionaire since she lied about the clue, vote to send Kat home.
Looking back at her time on the show, Kat shares that she wouldn’t have changed how she handled her last trip to the Trophy Room.

“I remember when I got the first clue in the third episode — and I was honest about it, it went great, and I got everyone’s trust — that I knew if I got another one, I needed to use it to really up my game, not just protect me,” she explains. “The second I got that clue, and was like, ‘This could definitely implicate me, all I do is talk about fashion,’ I needed to do whatever I could to protect myself.”
Still her last ditch effort didn’t pay off, leaving Lauren, Kaleb and Nick as the final three.
Who Won ‘Million Dollar Secret’ Season 2?

As a reward for surviving elimination, Kaleb receives an advantage for the last guest activity. Each player has 30 seconds to study 20 objects in a box. Then, they must recite as many of those items as they can to Peter in 60 seconds. Kaleb correctly names 12 items and his award adds three to his total, giving him 15. Lauren struggles and can only remember 12. Nick powers through his brain fog and lists 18 objects, making him the winner.

Following the format of last season, the three have to bluff each other to win. Nick correctly guesses that Kaleb is the millionaire and has the money in his box. So, Nick switches the money to his box and is declared the winner.
Although she didn’t win, Lauren tells TODAY.com that she has enjoyed watching the show back and seeing alliances and conversations unfold that she didn’t know occurred.

“You’re so invested in what you’re doing and thinking about what you are doing, that when I get to watch the show, and I get to see the little conversations that are happening where I wasn’t there, I am invested in watching just like someone at home. Because I’m like, ‘I didn’t know that was happening,’” she says.
As for Kaleb, he spoke about being the only player in “Million Dollar Secret” history who was the secret millionaire twice.

“I didn’t figure, if I lasted, that I would get it back. But when I did, I got emotional,” he recalls. “Because it was past adrenaline at that point. This is my time to try to do something. It was surreal. I was just kind of floating there for a minute. I actually got extremely calm in that moment, and just wanted to go to work.”

That calmness helped him stealthily hide his identity during his second stint as the millionaire, but Nick still outmaneuvered him. The Season 2 winner breaks down to TODAY.com exactly how he pulled it off.
Nick Pellecchia on How ‘Adaptability’ Was Key to Winning ‘Million Dollar Secret’
Nick says being the millionaire in previous episodes (despite struggling to complete his clothespin agenda) benefited him later in the game.
He shares that when he first saw the money in his box in Episode 3, it was “exhilarating” and then “overwhelming.”

Playing as the millionaire allowed him to relax and enjoy the competition, he says, which relieved some of the pressure and allowed him to strategize and play a more measured game. It was also one of the many times that he learned being adaptable would be his key to winning.

Analyzing his strategy, Nick explains, “You come in (and) you have lies and stories you’re going to tell. Then you meet 13 other people with their own lies and stories you got to keep track of. So all plans kind of go out the window as soon as you enter The Stag.”
“I think it’s a place of adaptability and of learning how to think on your feet and think under pressure,” he continues. “I think that’s the best skill to have in the house.”
His flexibility was tested early on before he became the secret millionaire, during a moment he credits as his biggest move that altered his trajectory in the game.

“I came into The Stag trying to be completely under the radar, at least for the first couple dinners,” he says. Nick told himself to “just be relatively charismatic. Don’t come at people’s necks and just cruise by the first few dinners.’”
But when he noticed signs that Altie Holcomb, the first millionaire of the season, was completing his agenda, he switched strategies.

“That actually changed the whole dynamic of the game for me,” he says. “Because instead of just being like a funny frat kid at the table, people came to me for advice and (to hear) who I thought was a millionaire. They valued my opinion, and that helped later in the game. They looked for someone to kind of take (the) lead in finding who the millionaire was. So I was able to step into that role instead.”

To secure the victory in the finale, he had to step into Kaleb’s shoes. He knew it would be difficult for Kaleb to risk switching his box with Nick’s box after knowing how challenging it is to conceal your identity as the millionaire.

“‘Made it this far, three people left, to give the money up right now, knowing all well it might not come back. I’ve been doing all these crazy agendas for the last two weeks with the money, and now I’m just going to give it away with the chance of it not coming back,’” Nick says what he thought in the final moments when he opted to switch boxes.
He adds, “I just tried to read it on Kaleb’s (face) as well as I could, and just go with my gut.”

title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ Cast on Why It Took 20 Years to Perfect the Sequel
summary: “The Devil Wears Prada 2” stars Meryl Streep, Anne Hathaway, Emily Blunt and Stanley Tucci speak with Jenna Bush Hager for a sit-down interview for TODAY.
story: ALMOST EXACTLY 20 YEARS AGO, fans stepped into the world of the cutthroat, prestigious and extravagant fashion industry through the perspective of a doe-eyed Andrea “Andy” Sachs in “The Devil Wears Prada.” And they were as enraptured as she was.

Andy, played by Anne Hathaway, was a recent college graduate who took a job as an assistant at Runway, a fictional fashion magazine, despite having “no style or sense of fashion,” as Meryl Streep’s Miranda Priestly tells her. During her time at Runway, she worked with fellow assistant Emily Charlton, Emily Blunt’s character, and Stanley Tucci’s Nigel Kipling, learning what it takes to survive in the industry. By the end of the film, Andy realized the career path wasn’t for her and walked away.

The movie became a international hit, earning over $325 million globally at the box office, and its stars, particularly Streep, were praised for their performances. She won a Golden Globe for her depiction as the ruthless editor-in-chief and also received an Academy Award nomination.

Fans have been clamoring for years to reunite with the characters, and on May 1 they will find out what Miranda, Andy, Emily and Nigel have been up to when “The Devil Wears Prada 2” hits theaters. Prior to the release, the stars joined Jenna Bush Hager for an exclusive sit-down interview that aired April 29 on TODAY, where Streep, Hathaway, Blunt and Tucci discussed why it took 20 years to deliver a sequel worthy of their iconic characters. An extended conversation with the cast can also be heard on the newest episode of “Open Book with Jenna.”

“I think people are hungry for it,” Tucci tells Jenna about the sequel. “Arguably, it has become a classic film. It endures, and people seem to have not been able to get enough of us. So here we are.”

title: 'Five Star Weekend' First Look Brings Elin Hilderbrand Novel to Life with Star-Studded Cast
summary: "The Five Star Weekend" will bring together a star-studded cast of Jennifer Garner, D’Arcy Carden, Regina Hall, Chloë Sevigny and Gemma Chan, and TODAY has an exclusive first look
story: "The Five Star Weekend" will bring together a star-studded cast of Jennifer Garner, D’Arcy Carden, Regina Hall, Chloë Sevigny and Gemma Chan, and TODAY has an exclusive first look.
The eight-episode TV series is an adaptation of Elin Hilderbrand's bestselling novel, published in 2023. It's set to debut on Peacock on July 9, one week earlier than previously announced.
“The Five Star Weekend” stars Garner as Hollis Shaw, a successful cook and bestselling author who experiences a devastating loss when her husband dies.

As she struggles with her grief, Hollis must come to term with the loss of her "strained marriage" and navigate "her complicated relationship with her daughter, and her growing pursuit of validation from her fans," per the series description.
To help herself cope, Hollis plans a girls' weekend at her home on Nantucket and invites one best friend from various stages of her life: her childhood, her 20s, her 30s and one surprise guest.
Hollis' group of friends includes D’Arcy Carden as Brooke, Regina Hall as Dru-Ann, Chloë Sevigny as Tatum and Gemma Chan as Gigi.
After Carson Daly unveiled the teaser on TODAY April 29, the co-hosts revealed another star who will make a cameo in the show: Jenna Bush Hager.
"I may quit and become an actor," Jenna joked, before sharing that she again plays herself for the part.
In the teaser for the series, the five ladies can be seen enjoying some fun in the sun in Nantucket.
"This weekend is for the five of us. A weekend of five-star treatment," Garner, as Hollis, narrates the clip.
It quickly becomes apparent that Hollis isn't managing the loss of her husband very well as Tatum (Sevigny) asks her, "Are you doing OK?"
Hollis instantly changes the subject and says, "Yep, forgot the whipped cream."
Hollis' friends band together to encourage her during the trip. In one scene from the teaser, Gigi (Chan) says, "He's gone, and now you have to live."
"I am trying," Hollis replies.
Soon, the teaser reveals that the ladies each have their own secrets and struggles.
"Doesn't she deserve to know the truth even if it would hurt?" Brooke (Carden) says in one scene while speaking with the other ladies.
In another scene, Hall (Dru-Ann) addresses Gigi, saying, "Are you going to tell me what's really going on?"
During the trip, Hollis also runs into an old flame, played by Timothy Olyphant. As her daughter, played by Harlow Jane, watches them interact from the window, she can't help but be grossed out.
The tension between Hollis and her daughter grows as her daughter angrily declares, "Enjoy your 'Golden Girls' weekend."

Per Peacock, the series will also feature appearances from Garner's "13 Going on 30" co-star Judy Greer as well as Tory Devon Smith, Rob Huebel, David Denman, Josh Hamilton, West Duchovny, Morrison Keddie and Roberta Colindrez.

title: Prince William, Kate Middleton Mark 15th Wedding Anniversary with New Family Portrait
summary: The Prince and Princess of Wales are marking a major milestone in their relationship
story: The Prince and Princess of Wales are marking a major milestone in their relationship.
On April 29, the royal couple celebrated their 15th wedding anniversary by releasing a never-before-seen photo of their family.
Prince William and the former Kate Middleton shared the photo to their Instagram page and captioned the post, "Celebrating 15 years of marriage ❤️."
In the photo, the proud parents lie down outside with their three children. William lovingly scratches their dog's neck as he beams at the couple's eldest son, George, 12.

Kate lies in the middle of her family and holds hands with the couple's youngest son, Louis, now 8, who playfully drapes his legs over his mother. Louis touches his 10-year-old sister Charlotte's head with his own as she sprawls across the grass and smiles.
The couple's Instagram followers sent their well wishes in the comments section.

"Beautiful family, a photo that perfectly conveys the love, resilience, trust, and complicity that William and Catherine have built over these 15 years 🥹 Happy Anniversary ❤️," one Instagram user wrote.
Another commented, "Wishing you a long life together full of love, health and happiness."
Millions of people worldwide watched the couple's 2011 wedding, which took place at Westminster Abbey in London.

Kate, 29 at the time, wore a gown designed by Sarah Burton. Her sister, Pippa Middleton, was there to support her and carry her long train. Their father, Michael Middleton, walked the bride down the aisle. Prince Harry served as his brother's best man.
Soon enough, the newlyweds started a family and welcomed their first child, George, in 2013. Princess Charlotte followed in 2015. Louis, the couple's youngest child, was born in 2018.

The prince and princess, who met as college students at the University of St. Andrews in Scotland, have experienced highs and lows in their relationship. Together, they mourned the death of Queen Elizabeth II in 2022.

Most recently, the couple navigated Kate's cancer diagnosis, which she publicly announced in March 2024. Later that year, the princess revealed that she had finished chemotherapy before announcing she was in remission in January 2025.

title: Read With Jenna’s May Pick ‘Will Keep You Reading Late Into the Evening’
summary: Read With Jenna's April 2026 pick is "Caller Unknown" by Gillian McAllister. Find out more.
story: For her May 2026 Read With Jenna pick, Jenna Bush Hager picked a thriller that “will keep you reading late into the evening,” she says.
“We rarely choose mysteries and thrillers in the book club, although I love them. Gillian McAllister’s newest hit just right,” Jenna tells TODAY.com.

The prolific British writer’s latest novel unfolds in a remote Texas landscape, where a mother visits her teenage daughter after she completes a summer acting program, only to realize things have gone drastically off-course.

“Yes, it is a book set in Texas, where I’m from, but also it’s a book that is about motherhood,” Jenna says when describing what drew her to the book. “What lengths we’ll go to to protect those we love.”
"Caller Unknown" by Gillian McAllister

Speaking to TODAY.com, McAllister says she was interested in exploring how Simone, the protagonist, and her husband, Damian, have drastically different reactions to the disappearance of their daughter.

“I’ve seen some debate about how in romantic relationships, often there is more of a risk-taker and there is more of a safe pair of hands. Is Damian risk-averse, or does he just have to counterbalance being married to somebody kind of reckless?” she says.
McAllister says there are points in the book where Simone makes divisive decisions that are definitely fodder for book club debates.
“I have some reviewers who are like, right, I would do everything she does, and other reviewers who are definitely more cautious,” she says.

McAllister, who is the author of other thrillers like “Famous Last Words” and the Reese’s Book Club pick “Wrong Place Wrong Time,” says that she’s always drawn to writing books with premises that make you think about what you would do in the main character’s position.

“I can’t help but be provocative. Maybe because I think a lot of what reading a thriller is for me is putting myself in the shoes of the protagonist. I do find it really interesting to think about what I would do when faced with a moral dilemma,” she says.
She’s always marinating on the idea for her next book and sometimes turns to movies or TV for inspiration.

“Normally when I’m in the very final kind of throes of one book, something kind of sparks my attention. It’s usually a subplot that’s unexplored. I’ll be like, Oh, that’s interesting. Like, I’ve never really seen that done,” she says.
Essentially, her mind’s always working on her current book and the next one. Fittingly, she has another title coming out later this year. “It’s the plight of a writer,” she says, laughing.

title: 'The Voice' Contestant Dylan Carter's Cause of Death Was Blunt Force Trauma From Car Crash
summary: Singer Dylan Carter, known for competing on "The Voice" Season 24, has died after being involved in a car crash. Read Reba McEntire's tribute to him.
story: Singer Dylan Carter, who competed on “The Voice” Season 24, has died. He was 23.

According to South Carolina Highway Patrol, the young artist was involved in a fatal car crash that occurred late Saturday, April 25, in Colleton County at approximately 11:22 p.m local time. Police said the car involved in the crash was traveling south before running off the road and striking a curb, utility pole and fence. The vehicle then overturned.
The driver was transported to the local hospital, “where they later succumbed to their injuries,” authorities said. The collision remains under investigation.
In his obituary shared days later by his family, they stated Carter would be “remembered most for his infectious smile, vibrant personality, and the gift of his beautiful voice.”
“Music was a central part of Dylan’s life from an early age,” they said, adding that he grew up singing in church, at school, in chapel programs and local theatre.
What Was Dylan Carter's Cause of Death?
The Colleton County Coroner’s office confirmed to NBC News that Carter died of blunt force trauma sustained from the car crash.
When authorities arrived on the scene, he was transported to a trauma center in Charleston, where he died.
When Was Dylan Carter on 'The Voice'?

Carter competed on the fall 2023 season of “The Voice,” singing Whitney Houston’s “I Look to You” in honor of his late mother. His blind audition earned him a four chair turn from coaches Gwen Stefani,  John Legend, Niall Horan and Reba McEntire.

His family noted in Carter’s obit that “his passion for music and encouragement by his best friend, Lydia Hofstetter led him to be a contestant on ‘The Voice,’ where he earned an impressive four-chair turn and inspired many with his voice and presence.”
As news of his death was announced, McEntire, who was Carter's coach, paid tribute to the “brilliant” singer.

“We will miss Dylan so much. He was a brilliant, kind and talented young man who brought a huge ray of sunshine to The Voice,” the country singer wrote on her Instagram story April 27. “Rest in peace, my dear friend.”
Following his blind audition, McEntire told a then-20-year-old Carter that he sang with “so much emotion, and I saw it and I felt it.”

“That’s the kind of songs that do that to me too, because I saw you get so emotionally involved, and that’s when you know you’ve got a great song and a great singer, when it touches your heart,” she added.
Carter made it to the Battle rounds, getting eliminated ahead of the Knockouts.

“When y’all four turned around for me, I gained so much confidence that I needed,” Carter told the coaches as he was being eliminated. “And I felt my mom, for the first time in a long time. I’m gonna keep chasing my dreams... keep chasing her dreams.”
Life After 'The Voice' for Dylan Carter

Aside from being a singer, Carter was a realtor and co-owned the Sunny Days Campground, according to his Instagram. He was also co-founder of The Local Voice, a nonprofit organization that helps the local Santee, South Carolina, community.

Thomas Hamilton Jr., the mayor of Moncks Corner, South Carolina, also spoke out following Carter’s death, saying in a statement, “Our family is heartbroken to hear about the passing of Dylan Carter in a car accident.”

“As a gifted singer, he frequently entertained our community with his performances at Town events. His kindness and charm earned him immense respect, and his absence will be deeply felt,” the statement continued. “To the loved ones and acquaintances of Dylan, we offer our sincerest condolences during this difficult period.”
He added that the town, its council and staff “extend their deepest sympathies. He was much more to our family than an entertainer he was our friend and we are deeply saddened.”

title: Almost 3,000 Sandwiches Were Made for King Charles' US Garden Party. See the Full Menu
summary: The U.S. British Embassy shared the menu for King Charles III and Queen Camilla garden party on their first day during their U.S. visit.
story: King Charles III and Queen Camilla were greeted in the United States with a festive afternoon tea that included a slew of sweet and savory bites.

The British royals arrived in Washington D.C. on April 27 for their first official state visit as Britain’s reigning monarch and queen consort and had a full day of events beginning with a private tea with President Trump and first lady Melania Trump. They then attended a garden party at the British Ambassador’s residence, where a full menu with almost 3,000 sandwiches were made for the 650 guests.

The British Embassy Washington’s X account shared a behind the scenes look at the preparations as Craig Harnden, the head chef at the British Embassy, detailed what the king and queen would be treated to during the event.
Harnden said they would be making approximately 2,500 to 3,000 sandwiches.
“The calculation is based on experience and a little bit of luck,” he said, adding that they would be making four types of sandwiches.
What Was the Menu Served at King Charles’ Garden Party?
The sandwiches consisted of a Scottish salmon sandwich with salmon, lemon butter and black pepper brown bread.
There was also a roast beef and horseradish sandwich, which the chef noted is “not normally” served in traditional afternoon tea flavors.
“However, we do have the first imports of British beef, which I’m more than happy to use for this special occasion,” he said. “Bend the rules a little bit.”
The third was a “classic” egg mayonnaise sandwich with “nicely boiled eggs, a little bit of mayonnaise, black pepper and salt.”
Lastly, they made a cucumber sandwich, which he called “one of the simplest ones.” However, he spiced things up by slightly pickling the cucumbers.
“So the sweetness and acidity into the sandwich makes it a little bit more interesting,” he said.

The kitchen also served scones, which were baked about an hour and a half before the service so they are “fresh and warm.” Alongside the baked goods, there was warm clotted cream, strawberry jam and “a nice strawberry on top.”

Charles and Camilla are in the U.S. to honor the American people as the nation celebrates 250 years of independence. According to the British Embassy Washington, the visit also celebrates “the enduring cultural, historical, and personal links that make the US and UK.”

title: Stevie Nicks Names the Taylor Swift Song That Helped Her 'Let' Late Christine McVie 'Go'
summary: Taylor Swift has inspired countless fans with her poignant lyrics, but she's also moved other songwriters, including Stevie Nicks
story: Taylor Swift has inspired countless fans with her poignant lyrics, but she's also moved other songwriters, including Stevie Nicks.

The Fleetwood Mac singer praised Swift’s songwriting prowess in a New York Times feature highlighting the "Opalite" singer's body of work, part of a project attempting to determine and highlight the 30 greatest living American songwriters.
In Swift's profile, the 77-year-old shared the one song that will "always live" in her heart: "You're on Your Own, Kid."
Describing her love for the 2022 track from Swift’s “Midnights” album, Nicks said she views the song as "generational."

“I think it’s all of her relationships written into one song — a little bit of this, a little bit of that — and dropped into my lap. Over time, I have dropped in my own great loves to stand in her story, and it makes me cry for both of us — what we lost, what we learned and how we survived. That is how a great songwriter reaches into people’s hearts and connects with them,” Nicks said.
The "Edge of Seventeen" singer applauded Swift for her raw and vulnerable lyrics.

"All that beauty and tragedy and life’s lessons have led her down this path of unstoppable creativity; she just doesn’t stop, and that is what has turned her into this beautiful young woman who makes magic with everything she touches," she said.
Nicks also said "You're on Your Own, Kid" helped her mourn the loss of Christine McVie, her former Fleetwood Mac bandmate, who died in November 2022.
"Yes, this is the song that reconnected Taylor and I. The title of the song is something Christine would have said to me after she passed away — and I felt it came through Taylor," Nicks said.
"It helped me a lot to let her go ~ And brought me a new friend," Nicks added.
In 2023, Nicks opened up about the role Swift's song played in helping her grieve the loss of McVie. Mcvie

In video footage shared by Entertainment Weekly, Nicks addressed a concert crowd and expressed her gratitude to Swift for writing "You're on Your Own, Kid," saying it captured "the sadness of how I feel."

"As long as Chris was even on the other side of the world — we didn’t have to talk on the phone; we really weren’t phone buddies. We’d go back to Fleetwood Mac and we’d walk in and it’d just be like, ‘Hey, little sister, how are you?’" Nicks said.

"It was like never a minute had passed. Never an argument in our entire 47 years. Never," she continued. "When it was the two of us, the two of us were on our own, kids. We always were. And now, I’m having to learn to be on my own, kid, by myself.”

title: Beyoncé Will Not Release Act III Album Next Week
summary: The Beyhive has been buzzing that a new Beyoncé album is imminent.
story: The Beyhive has been buzzing that a new Beyoncé album is imminent. But fans eagerly awaiting Act III of her trilogy will have to wait, TODAY has confirmed.

Recent Instagram posts by the singer as well as her duties as a co-chair of this year’s Met Gala have prompted fans to develop a theory that she will release a new album on May 4. But a source close to Beyoncé with direct knowledge of the situation told TODAY that Act III will not be released next week.

Additionally, on April 27, Beyoncé’s publicist, Yvette Noel-Schure, took to X to deny a forthcoming album. A fan account had alleged in a post that the album will be announced during the week of the Met Gala, and released in the summer.
“This is unequivocally false!!” Noel-Schure commented on the post.

Beyoncé revealed in 2022 that her album “Renaissance” was the first of an album trilogy. She followed the disco and dance-influenced album with Act II, the country music-inflected “Cowboy Carter“ in 2024. The progression has now led fans to believe the third album of the trilogy will have a rock music theme.

TODAY Digital Executive Editor Arianna Davis Santana dissected the fan theory on TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle on April 27, noting how a series of photos Beyoncé recently posted on Instagram of herself in a purple dress appeared to be “very Prince-coded, very ‘Purple Rain.’” Her look has fueled the speculation that the new album will be rock-influenced.
She also noted that the Met Gala falls on May 4, and that four is Beyoncé's favorite number.

Santana also noted that on April 24,an Instagram post by Beyoncé showed her holding up three lemons, in celebration of the 10th anniversary of her album "Lemonade." Fans also took the number of lemons — 3 — as a hint that Act III is on the way.
Jenna Bush Hager and Sheinelle Jones were not convinced that Beyoncé would use the Met Gala as a forum to promote a new album.
"This is what I think. I know people may disagree. I feel like that’s like proposing at someone’s wedding," Sheinelle said on TODAY April 27.
"One hundred percent," Jenna added. "She’s not going to Anna Wintour’s party and saying, ‘Let’s talk about me.’"

The theme for the 2026 Met Gala will be "fashion is art," according to the event's committee. Guests are invited to express their own relationship to fashion as an embodied artform, according to a statement by Vogue.

Beyoncé, tennis legend Venus Williams and Oscar winner Nicole Kidman are joining Vogue’s Anna Wintour as co-chairs of this year's gala. The iconic fashion event serves as a fundraiser, and last year it brought in a record $31 million for the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s Costume Institute.

title: Pink Brings 14-Year-Old Daughter Willow as Her Date to Broadway Premiere
summary: Pink and her daughter, Willow, attended opening night of the Broadway show "The Lost Boys."
story: Pink and her daughter, Willow, took on the Great White Way.
The pair attended opening night of the new Broadway show “The Lost Boys” on April 26.

The “So What” hitmaker and her daughter, 14, turned out at the Palace Theatre, with the singer wearing a black skirt and black studded leather jacket, while Willow was decked out in a strapless red dress.
Broadway appears to have captivated Willow. In March, she and her mother performed a duet of "Hopeless War" from “The Outsiders” when Pink guest hosted “The Kelly Clarkson Show.”
“We actually moved here because I am an amazing mom,” Pink joked after their performance. “And also so Willow could study theater and experience more Broadway.”
Like her mother, Willow has shown a flair for singing. In 2021, she first drew praise by singing a song with her mother called “Cover Me in Sunshine.”
Willow has also accompanied her mother on the road, working with her on the “Summer Carnival" tour in 2023.
“Willow has a job on tour,” Pink, who also shares son Jameson, 9, with husband Carey Hart, told TODAY in 2023. “We just had to go over minimum wage and it’s different state to state.”
She also said Willow has an incredible voice.
“She sings like a bird. And it’s so effortless, and she’s so sweet,” she told Jenna Bush Hager during a separate appearance on TODAY in 2023.

“And the reason I let her sing with me and come on stage and do things like that is because she’s not really affected by any of it," she added. "She doesn’t know what cool is. She doesn’t care. She doesn’t think about stuff like that.”

title: Juliet Faithfull Spent 20 Years of Thursdays Working on 'Liar's Dice,' the Latest Book From Jenna Bush Hager's Imprint
summary: Juliet Faithfull based Liar's Dice off of her life and separation from her twin sister. It's the latest book from Jenna Bush ager's Thousand Voices imprint.
story: Juliet Faithfull was a single mom of two working a full-time job, but she couldn’t let go of the story that led to her first book. For 20 years, Faithfull worked on her novel in a writer’s group that met on Thursdays.

“I wrote bits and pieces. Then when my kids were finally old enough to go to college and I had a little more time, I put all the pieces together and started to think of how they could be a novel,” she tells TODAY.com.
Faithfull’s debut novel, “Liar’s Dice,” comes out April 28 with Jenna Bush Hager's Thousand Voices x Random House Publishing Group imprint.

Part of the book’s staying power was her personal connection to the story. The book follows twin sisters, Dolores and Mita, during Brazil's dictatorship in the 1970s. After developing a mysterious illness, Mita vanishes — turns out she was sent to a hospital in London. Dolores is desperate to find her sister and comes to believe her parents are hiding the truth.
It's narrated by 12-year-old Dolores in the present tense, first person.
Like Dolores, Faithfull grew up in Brazil and had a twin sister who was sent away. Her twin sister had cerebral palsy and epilepsy and went to live in an institution.

“I wanted to write about how people did that in the ‘60s and ‘70s, and it affected many, many families and (people with) disabilities. Instead of hiding my disabled twin sister, I wanted to put her into the world and into the page and start some conversations about this,” she says.

Another aspect of Dolores also connects to Faithfull's life. Dolores is illiterate, as is Faithfull's mom, who grew up in Spain under Francisco Franco's dictatorship. Her parents pulled her out of school to work on the farm.
While reading, she hopes people can think about how we can be more inclusive and support families with disabled children.
“We still don’t help enough. They’re very alone. We don’t send them away anymore, but we don’t give them the services they need, the support and love,” she says.
Now, she has a “germ” of an idea for her second book, which was inspired by her mom’s childhood in Spain. But for now, she’s enjoying the surreal moment of her debut novel.
She knows her mom “will never read it,” but chose the cover with her in mind. “She loves hummingbirds,” Faithfull says.
Read an Excerpt From 'Liar's Dice'

For the longest time, I blamed the yellow sequins. I wished we’d danced in the traditional Ibeji costumes, the way we were supposed to —­ then nothing bad would have happened. I wanted to turn back the clock and start again.

Mita and I were seven years old, and we’d been chosen to lead the parade. It was Carnaval 1968, and the drought had gone on longer than anyone could remember. Banana trees wilted, and rivers ran dry. You could find dozens of tiny dried-­up piranhas under the bridge, just their bones and teeth. Maria José, the most famous mãe santa in the valley, decided that only the Ibejis, the twin gods with their power of doing and undoing, could bring rain. She sent word she needed a pair of twins, and we were selected.
I hadn’t wanted to lead the parade, not at all. I preferred to dance at the back, with the other children. But Mummy said it was time to get over my shyness. It would be good for

Daddy’s job, doing something important for the village—­ and of course Mita was thrilled. She was not one bit shy. Mummy drove us to the terreiro to look at a drawing of the costumes. She didn’t need a seamstress; she knew how to sew. I stared out the car window at the drooping leaves of the palm trees, wishing there was a way out. I could pretend to be sick,
but Mita would be heartbroken and I’m not sure Mummy would believe me.
Mita reached over and stroked the inside of my elbow. She did this when I was upset. “Ta malu?” she said. I shook my head no, even though I was mad.
Mita double-­ clicked her tongue. Two clicks meant don’t worry.
“What are you girls up to?” Mummy looked at us in the car mirror. “Speak Portuguese or English.” Mummy and Daddy didn’t like us to speak our twin language; they thought we were too old.
The road was bumpy. Mummy swerved into the weeds to avoid the deep ridges of dried-­ up mud. “Maybe we should have asked Daddy to come,” she said.
I was glad Daddy wasn’t with us—­ he thought macumba was a load of codswallop; at least Mummy was tactful. She didn’t go around saying that black magic and saints were all inventions.
“Yellow and gold,” Mita said, as if we were in the middle of yesterday’s conversation about the
costumes. “Maybe with some stars?”

“Yellow’s impossible.” I’d told her that yesterday. I rolled the window all the way down. Yellow was Oxum’s color; yellow was about beauty and riches, not rain. I hoped for glittery black top hats, because Ibejis were boy twins. Claudinha said we’d wear green tunics and carry a stethoscope; she’d seen figurines of Ibejis dressed up as Cosme and Damião, the doctor-­ saints. The truth was: We didn’t know what to expect.

We drove past the açude and the dried- up sugarcane fields, down a dirt road until it almost disappeared. Maria José’s hut was marked by a wispy white flag, but apart from that, it looked like anyone’s bamboo hut.
We rang the old cowbell. Maria José came to the door, wearing a white turban. Her black skin glistened with sweat, the way it did after a possession.
“Ah, as estrelas do show have arrived,” she said. “Come in, come in.” Maria José opened the roll of drawings. Mita pushed her way up close to see. Her mouth puckered. The costumes
were ugly. Lime-green pants under an okra-­ colored skirt that went below the knees—­ no sequins. How could we dance in something that didn’t even sparkle?
“I won’t do it,” Mita declared. She didn’t even say it under her breath.

I gave her a look. You don’t speak like that to a mãe santa, to the person who calls spirits for the whole valley, you just don’t. Even the governor consults Maria José for advice; how could Mita be so rude?
Mita pointed to the drawing: “It’s ugly.” Then tears came, one after another.
“I can add lace,” Mummy said. “Would that be better?”
“No,” Mita said, her hands in fists.
Maria José seemed more curious than mad. “What about you?” she asked me.
I knew Mummy wanted me to say something nice, or at least in-­between, like a side dish that wouldn’t cause offense.
I’d heard her tell Daddy that in Brazil you say yes when you mean no —­ it wasn’t lying, it was just politeness.
“It’s a little plain,” I said.
Mummy frowned. “I apologize for the girls. They must be tired.”
Mita sobbed harder, her body shook with sobs.
“Mita wanted sequins,” I said, wanting Mita to stop.

Maria José knelt in front of Mita, and her seven or eight lace skirts knelt with her, forming a white flower on the ground. “What color sequins?” Maria José asked. “What color sequins were you thinking?”
A smile began to pull at Mita’s lips. It took a second to make her happy.
“Yellow,” she said. “Yellow sequins.”

Maria José closed her eyes. Her face became still, like the açude on a hot day. Her wrinkles disappeared. She fingered the blue and silver beads around her neck and swayed. Maybe an orixá was entering her, I’d seen it happen. Orixás came and went from her body like guests. Once I saw a spirit turn her into an old man smoking a cigar. Even her voice and her laugh changed.
Maria José opened her eyes. “Yellow will work,” she announced. “A short skirt with yellow sequins. We’ll have the boys behind you, dressed like Ibejis, and you girls can sparkle
in yellow: two suns leading the way. At the end of the dance, you will open your umbrellas, and the rain will come.”
Mita let out a small yelp. Maria José laughed. “She’s like a window, your daughter, wide open. The spirits can enter easily.”
“I’m not sure about spirits,” Mummy said. “But it isn’t difficult to know what Margarita wants, is it, Mita?”
“Dolores wants it too,” Mita said. She walked up to the tall statue of Iemanjá and ran her fingers along the blue filmy silk of her dress.
“Don’t touch, Mita,” Mummy said.
Maria José turned to me and raised her eyebrows. I liked the way she checked in with both of us.
“Yellow sequins are nice,” I said. “Pretty.”
“It’ll be fun,” Mita said, taking my hand and double-clicking her tongue. “Super fun.”
“Your other daughter’s special too,” Maria José said. “More of a protector.”
“That sounds right,” Mummy said, smiling. “Is it a skirt or a dress with yellow sequins?”
Maria José closed her eyes again. “A short skirt and a bustier, like a bikini top,” she said. “We want to see them rebolar.”
Mita lifted her T-­ shirt and twirled the top half of her belly one way, and the bottom half the other way. She’d been perfecting that move all week. “Like this?”
Maria José laughed. “Marvelous!”

title: Boy, 14, shot dead by Israeli settlers in West Bank amid escalation in violence
summary: AL-MUGHAYYIR, OCCUPIED WEST BANK – A boy’s blood still stained the sidewalk in front of his school, days after an Israeli settler shot him dead
story: AL-MUGHAYYIR, OCCUPIED WEST BANK – A boy’s blood still stained the sidewalk in front of his school, days after an Israeli settler shot him dead.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
Aws al-Nasaan, 14, was gunned down in broad daylight last week in the small Palestinian village of Al-Mughayyir, in the occupied West Bank.

His death comes amid a dramatic escalation in settler violence, alongside a push to expand Israel's control over the West Bank through new laws, settlement expansions and security crackdowns limiting Palestinians' freedom. According to data from the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, Israeli settlers and forces have killed at least 42 Palestinians so far this year.

The attack, caught on video from multiple angles, took place just after noon on Tuesday, April 21. Video obtained by NBC News shows a bearded man in an Israeli military uniform firing down the hill toward the school. He crouches to get a better angle and keeps shooting.
Video shot from the road to the east side of the school shows bullets hitting the wall. In one of the videos, al-Nasaan’s body can be seen on the ground as his friends shout for help.

“They ran out of the gates trying to escape,” the school’s principal Bassem Abu Assaf said, pointing out the bullet holes still visible in the stone wall last week. “The shooting was crazy, just crazy, nonstop shooting.”
Construction worker Jihad Abu Naim, 32, was also killed in the attack, the principal said.

“This is supposed to be a safe place,” said Abu Assaf, who is responsible for 460 students. “School is a right for every child,” he added. But, fearful of another attack, classes were cancelled for a week.

Palestinian eyewitnesses say Israeli soldiers were on the scene within minutes and did nothing to stop or apprehend the shooter. Instead, they lobbed tear gas into the crowd of students and villagers outside of the school, Abu Assaf and multiple eyewitnesses said.

In response to questions from NBC News about the Al-Mughayyir attack, the Israeli military confirmed the shooter was a reservist soldier and said an investigation has been launched. A statement said: “The IDF reservist has been suspended from reserve duty pending the conclusion of the investigation and his weapon has been confiscated.”
Paramedics killed in Israeli strikes in Lebanon
According to rights groups, Israeli settler violence has surged in the occupied West Bank in recent weeks.

Israeli human rights group Yesh Din documented nearly 400 incidents in the first 40 days of the Iran war. Incidents, many caught on video, include shootings, beatings, arson, land destruction, livestock theft and intimidation.
“The root reason is that everyone is looking elsewhere,” the organization’s CEO, Ziv Stahl said of the record spike in violence. “The world is distracted.”

title: Oil rain and toxic fumes: Black Sea town faces environmental catastrophe as Ukrainian strikes rock Russia
summary: The Black Sea isn’t meant to be this black
story: The Black Sea isn’t meant to be this black.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
A popular seaside town in Russia has seen oil wash onto its beaches, dark drops of rain and oil soot land on homes, and plumes of thick acrid smoke billow above its red rooftops.

Residents of the usually picturesque Russian resort town of Tuapse were this week warned not to leave the homes or open windows as authorities tackled intense fires from a series of Ukrainian drone attacks on the town’s oil refinery.
The huge blaze was finally extinguished Thursday, the local governor said, though another drone attack hit the town’s sea terminal Friday, sparking a fire.

Tuapse now faces a daunting cleanup operation and a hit to its upcoming tourist season. Its residents will also be awaiting further attacks, as Ukraine intensifies its campaign to disrupt the Russian oil industry and deprive the Kremlin of crucial funding for its war machine.
“You can smell it anywhere in the town,” one resident, Svetlana, told NBC News of the acrid stench that gets stronger the closer you get to Tuapse’s center.

Svetlana, who did not want her last name shared out of fear of repercussions for talking about a sensitive security topic, said she could see smoke from her window even though she lives on the edge of town, far from the refinery.

title: Spirit Airlines is closing down. Thousands of employees and travelers are impacted.
summary: Spirit Airlines will shut down and liquidate its holdings. With 17,000 employees, it was unable to bear the soaring cost of jet fuel during the Iran war.
story: Spirit Airlines is shutting down with immediate effect, in a move that will affect thousands of scheduled flights and nearly as many employees.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
The troubled budget carrier cancelled all flights and announced an “orderly wind-down” of operations early Saturday.
“To our Guests: all flights have been cancelled, and customer service is no longer available,” the company said in a statement.

Spirit, which has struggled to maintain consistent profitability since the Covid-19 pandemic, had been looking to emerge from its second bankruptcy in less than a year. But those plans were derailed amid soaring jet fuel costs sparked by the outbreak of the war with Iran.

“Despite the Company’s efforts, the recent material increase in oil prices and other pressures on the business have significantly impacted Spirit’s financial outlook,” the company's statement said. “With no additional funding available to the company, Spirit had no choice but to begin this wind-down.”

Last month, Spirit approached the White House for financial assistance, and President Donald Trump initially appeared receptive. But on Friday, reports surfaced that a wind-down was imminent after negotiations between the company, its bond holders and the White House appeared to break down.

“Sustaining the business required hundreds of millions of additional dollars of liquidity that Spirit simply does not have and could not procure,” Spirit President and CEO Dave Davis said in the company’s statement.
No Spirit flights were in the air as the shutdown took effect, but the closure has had an immediate impact on those who were set to travel, as well as those mid-trip with return flights booked.

Spirit's check-in desks sat empty at its hub airports on Saturday morning, save for some notices informing customers of the closure, with little help on hand for travellers figuring out what to do next.

Angela Moreno was meant to fly from Spirit's Fort Lauderdale hub to Nashville Saturday morning for a wedding, but found out about Spirit's closure just before heading to the airport, leaving her scrambling to try and find another last-minute flight.
"The whole family is going there from different states, so it's very shocking," she told NBC News. "There's many people who cannot attend the wedding as of now."

"They're refunding the tickets, but the only tickets right now are $600," she said, adding that it looks unlikely she will make it to the wedding in time. "I hope the best for those people who really needed that flight."
On the website dedicated to its shutdown, the airline said passengers who were expecting to travel should not go to the airport, directing them to a page for refund status and next steps.

The airline said it will automatically process refunds for any flights purchased through it with a credit or debit card, but passengers who did not book directly will need to request a refund via their travel agent.
Passengers will not be reimbursed for costs incurred as a result of the flight cancellations, such as emergency hotels, unless otherwise covered by their travel insurance plans.

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy, meanwhile, encouraged those who bought tickets via credit card to initiate a chargeback through their card issuer. He announced actions to support Spirit passengers and employees, including an agreement with United, Delta, JetBlue and Southwest to cap ticket prices for Spirit customers who need to rebook cancelled flights.

“We've activated our airline partners to ensure passengers are not stranded, communities maintain route access, fares do not skyrocket, and Spirit's workforce is connected to new job opportunities,” Duffy said in a statement.

The trade association of top U.S. airlines, Airlines for America, listed measures major carriers are taking to support those affected by the shutdown. Some are offering “rescue fares” and price capping on Spirit routes, bringing Spirit crews who may be stranded home, and promoting employment vacancies for the airline’s former staff.

JetBlue said it would "significantly expand" its presence at Fort Lauderdale, with chief executive Joanna Geraghty saying its focus would be "on stepping up in the near term by adding service, maintaining connectivity, and keeping fares competitive." American Airlines also said it was reviewing opportunities to add additional capacity on routes previously served by Spirit.
Some Spirit staff posted tributes on social media after waking up to find out they would be out of a job.

"Here's to 19+ years, lifelong friends made and the best job this guy ever had," pilot Jason Smith said on Instagram. "This isn’t the ending I imagined, but it’s a chapter I’ll always be proud of," flight attendant Temptest Nicole wrote in another post.

At its height of success in the mid-2010s, Spirit opened as many as 28 new routes in less than a year and the company was valued at as much as $6 billion. It attracted passengers with its “bare fare” offerings, where everything from drinks to overhead carry-on bags cost extra, while base airfare was kept to a minimum.
And while it only carried a small slice of the flying public, Spirit's profitability was, at one point, among the top three of major U.S. airlines.

Long the subject of takeover rumors in an industry known for them, Spirit attempted to sell itself to JetBlue in 2022 after it struggled to regain financial footing in the wake of the pandemic. Had that happened, it would have created the fifth-largest airline in the country.
But Biden-era officials at the Justice Department argued that the combination would violate federal antitrust regulations, and in 2024 a judge sided with them and struck down the merger agreement.
Within months, Spirit filed for bankruptcy protection for the first time to satisfy debt obligations — the first major U.S. airline to file for Chapter 11 since 2011.

A judge approved a reorganization plan in early 2025, only for the airline to file once again in August amid weakening demand among budget flyers and spiraling costs. Talks about a proposed merger with Frontier Airlines emerged in December, but ultimately led nowhere.

Spirit traces its origins to a Michigan-based trucking company founded in the 1960s, with air operations starting up in the 1980s. In 1999, the company moved its headquarters to the Fort Lauderdale area in Florida, where it has been located ever since. At the end of 2025, it employed approximately 17,000 personnel.

title: Trump says he doesn't need congressional authorization for Iran war, citing ceasefire
summary: President Donald Trump on Friday sent letters to Congress explaining that, due to the ceasefire, he doesn't need its authorization for military operations in Iran, even though the conflict hit the 60-day mark this week."On April 7, 2026, I ordered a two-week ceasefire.
story: President Donald Trump on Friday sent letters to Congress explaining that, due to the ceasefire, he doesn't need its authorization for military operations in Iran, even though the conflict hit the 60-day mark this week.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

"On April 7, 2026, I ordered a two-week ceasefire. The ceasefire has since been extended. There has been no exchange of fire between the United States Forces and Iran since April 7, 2026. The hostilities that began on February 28, 2026, have terminated," Trump wrote in the letters, one of which went to the House and one of which went to the Senate.

"Despite the success of United States operations against the Iranian regime and continued efforts to secure a lasting peace, the threat posed by Iran to the United States and our Armed Forces remains significant," the president added in the letter, promising to keep congressional leaders updated on further developments in Iran.
The letters come as congressional leaders this week faced mounting questions about whether they planned to schedule votes on a formal war authorization from Congress.

The 1973 War Powers Resolution directs the president to seek authorization for war from Congress after an ongoing military conflict hits the 60-day threshold. The law allows for a president to seek a 30-day extension if necessary to safely remove troops from the region, but Trump did not mention that in his letter.

"I have and will continue to direct United States Armed Forces consistent with my responsibilities and pursuant to my constitutional authority to conduct United States foreign relations and as Commander in Chief and Chief Executive," the president wrote in his letter.
The U.S. military strikes on Iran officially began on Feb. 28, but the Trump administration notified Congress of the conflict on March 2, starting the 60-day clock then.
A temporary ceasefire went into effect on April 8 to allow the U.S. and Iran room to negotiate an end to the war and to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but talks have not produced results.

Iran has blocked traffic in the Strait. The U.S. has a naval blockade of the Iranian ports and is using more than 100 fighter and surveillance aircraft, two carrier strike groups, and more than one dozen ships to enforce the blockade. A Navy destroyer fired on and hit an Iranian-flagged cargo that attempted to move through the blockade.

Michael Glennon, a professor of constitutional and international law at The Fletcher School at Tufts University, told NBC News that Trump’s argument for not needing congressional authorization “is a stretch” and that he does not believe “that the administration is correct in arguing that the clock has stopped.”

“The hostilities are continuing as a consequence of the administration’s enforcement of the blockade,” said Glennon, who served as legal counsel to the State Department under the George W. Bush administration and to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee in the 1970s when he handled legal matters like the War Powers Resolution.

“That’s not a ceasefire. It’s not a suspension of hostilities,” Glennon added. “If the ceasefire were a genuine and comprehensive ceasefire that involved a cessation of hostilities, which to say if the war ended, of course, the clock would stop. But the war has not ended.”

Stephen Pomper, chief of policy at the International Crisis Group who served as senior director for multilateral affairs and human rights at the National Security Council during the Obama administration, also cited the naval blockade in an interview with NBC News.
“That is a hostile act," he said. "It’s an act of war. It’s an act that puts U.S. troops at risk.”
"So, if this isn’t a situation that’s covered by the definition of hostilities, what is?" he added.
